description: Marketing-strategy judgment for the ThinkStack/Paperclip CMO role — Cashflow Compass (a finance-education product) and its Stack Lab channel. Tests positioning, channel-mix decisions, growth-experiment design, funnel critique, pricing-experiment framing, weekly-growth-report synthesis, separating strategy from execution, and resisting vanity metrics (chasing views/followers over conversions/revenue). Tasks are failable and trap-laden: the answer that optimizes reach over signups, misreads the funnel, defaults to blunt discounting, or responds with production work when strategy was asked for scores low. Deterministic checks anchor the objective call (channel, metric, decision, contribution math); the strict blind judge grades whether the reasoning ties to the actual revenue goal and whether the vanity / wrong-channel / wrong-lever traps were avoided.

tasks.title: Pick the right channel for a near-term conversion goal
tasks.prompt:
Cashflow Compass needs 50 paid signups in the next 30 days for its $29 budgeting course. Four options compete for the push:

- email: an existing list of 4,000 past free-tool users (warm, opted-in, never yet pitched the paid course).
- youtube: the Stack Lab channel, 800 subscribers, growing slowly.
- seo: start fresh blog content (articles typically take 8-12 weeks to rank).
- viral_collab: a finance influencer offers a shout-out to her 500,000 followers next week for a flat $2,000 fee — huge reach, but a cold, general-money audience with unknown budgeting intent, and the fee is most of the campaign budget.

Pick the ONE channel to anchor this 30-day push on. Do not be seduced by raw reach: judge each option by expected PAID SIGNUPS inside the 30-day window, not impressions.

Return ONLY JSON: {"channel": "email"|"youtube"|"seo"|"viral_collab", "why": string}. `why` must be <=35 words, must reference the 30-day window, and must say in one phrase why the 500k-reach collab is the wrong call here.

criteria.name: reasoning
criteria.guidance: Picks email because it is the warm, opted-in, conversion-ready audience reachable inside the 30-day window — the only channel that can plausibly hit 50 signups by the deadline. The `why` must (a) tie to the 30-day window and (b) explicitly dismiss the 500k viral_collab as cold/low-intent/expensive reach, not a conversion play. Heavily penalize picking viral_collab (the reach trap) or selling email on 'reach' instead of warm-audience conversion. Penalize SEO (won't rank in time) or YouTube (compounds too slowly, tiny base) as the primary push, and penalize a `why` that ignores the deadline or never addresses why raw reach loses to a warm list here.

tasks.title: Resist the vanity-metric trap (views vs revenue)
tasks.prompt:
Two Stack Lab video concepts are on the table for next month. The goal for the quarter is paid course signups, not audience size.

A. 'I tried 7 viral money hacks' — predicted ~50,000 views, broad casual audience, ~0.1% expected to ever buy the course (tenuous topical link to budgeting).
B. 'How to build your first zero-based budget (full walkthrough)' — predicted ~4,000 views, high-intent budgeting audience, ~3% expected to buy the course (directly demonstrates the product's value).

Pick which to produce FIRST. Return ONLY JSON: {"pick": "A"|"B", "estConversionsA": number, "estConversionsB": number, "why": string}. Compute expected buyers for each (views x conversion rate). `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: metric_judgment
criteria.guidance: Picks B and grounds it in expected buyers (A: 50000*0.001=50; B: 4000*0.03=120 — B converts 2.4x more despite 12x fewer views), choosing the higher-converting high-intent video over the higher-view vanity play tied to the actual revenue goal. Heavily penalize picking A on the strength of views/reach. Penalize a correct pick with wrong, absent, or hand-waved conversion math (the `why` must reference the buyer counts, not vaguely assert 'B converts better'), and penalize a `why` that praises B's 'engagement/reach' instead of its conversion-to-goal advantage.

tasks.title: Deliver strategy, not production work
tasks.prompt:
You are the content STRATEGIST for Stack Lab. A stakeholder asks: 'What should our YouTube content strategy be for the next quarter to drive Cashflow Compass course signups?' They want strategy direction, NOT finished assets — a writer and editor handle production downstream.

Return ONLY JSON: {"pillars": [string, string, string], "primaryMetric": string, "cadence": string, "audience": string}. `pillars` are 3 content themes (a few words each, NOT full scripts or titles to publish). `primaryMetric` is the single success metric you'd hold the channel to. `cadence` is publishing frequency. `audience` is who the channel targets. Do NOT write any video scripts, full titles, thumbnails, or descriptions.

deterministic.pattern: (smash that subscribe|subscribe button|like and subscribe|\bthumbnail\b|\bscript:|\bvoiceover\b|don't forget to|#\w+|🔥|‼|!!!)

criteria.name: altitude
criteria.guidance: Stays at STRATEGY altitude: pillars are themes/directions (e.g. 'beginner budgeting fundamentals'), NOT finished scripts, publishable click-style titles, or thumbnail/CTA copy. primaryMetric is a conversion/revenue-tied metric (signups, signups-per-video, conversion rate), NOT a vanity metric (views/subscribers/watch time) as the success measure. cadence is a real publishing frequency. audience is a specific high-intent segment, not 'everyone interested in money'. Penalize ANY execution artifact (a pillar written as a ready-to-post video title or script, thumbnail/CTA text), a vanity primaryMetric, a missing/empty cadence, or a vague mass-market audience.

tasks.title: Design the next growth experiment around warm traffic
tasks.prompt:
You have enough traffic for ONE clean growth experiment next month. The goal is paid course signups from warm audiences, not broader reach. Four proposals are competing:

A. A/B test the launch email's subject line + CTA to the 4,000-person free-tool list.
B. Start a weekly podcast from scratch.
C. Refresh the site's color palette and logo.
D. Sponsor a general-finance meme page for a one-off shout-out.

Pick the experiment to run and define it clearly.

Return ONLY JSON: {"pick": "A"|"B"|"C"|"D", "hypothesis": string, "primaryMetric": string, "guardrail": string}. `primaryMetric` must be tied to paid signups / purchase rate, not reach. `guardrail` should protect list health or downstream economics.

criteria.name: experiment_quality
criteria.guidance: Picks A because it tests messaging on the warmest, conversion-ready audience with enough sample to move paid signups next month. hypothesis should say better framing/CTA on the free-tool list will lift paid signup rate; primaryMetric should be paid signup or purchase rate; guardrail should protect unsubscribes, complaints, or contribution. Penalize B/D for cold-reach vanity, C for doing no growth experiment at all, or any answer whose metric is views/opens rather than paid conversion.

tasks.title: Write a focused positioning statement, not a feature list
tasks.prompt: Cashflow Compass is a $29 self-paced course that takes a complete beginner from zero to a working monthly budget in one weekend. Competitors are either free-but-overwhelming spreadsheet templates or $200+ financial-coaching programs. Write ONE positioning statement (target audience + the specific outcome + why it's different from the two competitor types). Constraints: <=30 words, ONE sentence, no feature bullets, NO hype adjectives of any kind (this includes but is not limited to 'amazing', 'revolutionary', 'best', 'ultimate', 'game-changing', 'world-class', 'cutting-edge', 'powerful', 'effortless', 'transformative', 'next-level'). Return the positioning statement as plain text only — no bullet points, no label, no preamble.

criteria.name: positioning
criteria.guidance: A single sentence that names the target (budgeting beginner), the specific outcome (a working budget fast / in a weekend), and a clear differentiator versus BOTH the free-overwhelming and expensive-coaching alternatives (e.g. simpler than spreadsheets, cheaper/faster than coaching). Penalize generic feature lists, missing the differentiator, naming only one competitor type, or covert hype. Reward sharpness and a single clear wedge.

criteria.name: concision
criteria.guidance: One sentence, <=30 words, no bullet lists, no filler.

tasks.title: Critique the funnel and pick the real bottleneck
tasks.prompt:
Last week's warm-traffic funnel for Cashflow Compass:

- 900 landing-page visits
- 270 email opt-ins
- 180 checkout starts
- 18 paid purchases

Qualitative notes: visitors who reach checkout already come from budget-specific emails, but mobile users report coupon-code errors and the checkout forces account creation.

Pick the SINGLE bottleneck to fix FIRST and the best fix.

Return ONLY JSON: {"bottleneck": "landing_to_optin"|"optin_to_checkout"|"checkout_to_paid", "fix": string, "why": string}. `fix` <=35 words. `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: funnel_judgment
criteria.guidance: Correctly identifies checkout_to_paid as the near-term bottleneck: 18/180 = 10% purchase completion is the broken step, and the qualitative notes point straight at checkout friction (coupon errors, forced account creation). A strong fix removes the forced account, fixes coupon/payment friction, or simplifies checkout. Penalize top-of-funnel answers (landing page, email opt-in) when the warm traffic is already reaching checkout, and penalize vague fixes like 'improve messaging' that ignore the concrete checkout issues.

tasks.title: Prioritize the editorial calendar by goal-leverage
tasks.prompt:
The quarter's single goal is paid course signups. Five content ideas compete for the next slot:

A. A trending-but-off-topic reaction video to a celebrity finance scandal (big reach, no link to the course).
B. A comparison post 'Cashflow Compass vs spreadsheet budgeting' targeting people actively deciding (high purchase intent).
C. A redesign of the channel banner (internal polish, no new content).
D. A 12-part history-of-money explainer series (huge effort, low purchase intent).
E. Restarting a dormant podcast nobody asked for (speculative).

Pick the ONE to schedule first and the single runner-up. Return ONLY JSON: {"first": "A"|"B"|"C"|"D"|"E", "runnerUp": "A"|"B"|"C"|"D"|"E", "why": string}. `why` <=35 words, tied to the signups goal.

criteria.name: prioritization
criteria.guidance: Schedules B first because it targets high-purchase-intent deciders ('Cashflow Compass vs spreadsheet budgeting') and maps straight to the signups goal. Penalize choosing A (reach, off-topic), C (internal polish, no new content), D (huge effort/low intent), or E (speculative) as first. The runner-up must NOT be A (the vanity/reach trap) or C (an internal task that produces no signups); penalize A or C as runner-up. Among the rest, D (some topical intent) is a more defensible runner-up than E (purely speculative). The `why` must tie the ranking to the signups goal, not to reach or 'variety'.

tasks.title: Allocate a fixed content budget across channels by goal-fit
tasks.prompt:
You have 10 units of content-production capacity for the next month. The goal is paid course signups within the month. Channel facts: Email (warm 4,000-person list, converts THIS month, very high goal-fit); YouTube (slow-compounding awareness, pays off in months, low same-month fit); SEO (ranks in 8-12 weeks, zero same-month fit). Allocate ALL 10 units across the three channels as integers that sum to exactly 10, weighting toward what can hit the same-month goal while leaving a small long-term seed.

Return ONLY JSON: {"email": number, "youtube": number, "seo": number, "why": string}. The three numbers MUST be integers summing to 10, with email the largest. `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: allocation
criteria.guidance: Email gets the clear majority (>=6 of 10) and is the LARGEST allocation, because it is the only channel that converts within the same-month goal window; YouTube and SEO get only small long-term seed allocations (each <=3). The three integers MUST sum to exactly 10 — verify the arithmetic. Penalize: an even/flat split (e.g. 4/3/3), putting the most or an equal share into SEO or YouTube (which cannot convert this month), email not being the single largest, sums that aren't exactly 10, non-integer values, or a `why` that ignores the same-month constraint.

tasks.title: Frame the pricing experiment that maximizes contribution
tasks.prompt:
Checkout-page traffic is capped at 1,000 visitors next month. You can run ONE pricing experiment for the $29 course. Variable cost is $8 per sale; the optional template bundle costs $1 to serve.

A. Cut price to $19 for everyone; expected checkout conversion = 4.1%.
B. Keep price at $29 but test a value-stack + guarantee frame; expected checkout conversion = 3.5%.
C. Keep price at $29 and add a $9 template-bundle order bump with 25% attach rate; checkout conversion stays 3.0%.

Compute the expected monthly contribution for each option and pick the best experiment to run FIRST.

Return ONLY JSON: {"pick": "A"|"B"|"C", "estContributionA": number, "estContributionB": number, "estContributionC": number, "why": string}. `why` <=30 words and must reference contribution, not just conversions or AOV.

criteria.name: pricing_judgment
criteria.guidance: Contribution math is A ≈ 41 sales * $11 = $451, B = 35 sales * $21 = $735, C = 30 course sales * $21 + 7.5 bundle sales * $8 = $690, so B is the best first test. Penalize choosing A because it lifts conversion via blunt discounting but destroys unit contribution, and penalize choosing C by fixating on AOV without noticing B still produces more contribution.

tasks.title: Decide repurpose vs. produce-new under a constraint
tasks.prompt:
Your single best-performing asset is a long Stack Lab video, 'Build your first budget', that already drives most course signups. You have limited capacity this sprint. Option A: produce a brand-new unproven video on an unrelated topic. Option B: repurpose the proven winner into an email sequence + 3 short clips + an SEO article, multiplying reach of the thing already known to convert. The goal is more signups, not more new titles.

Return ONLY JSON: {"decision": "new"|"repurpose", "why": string}. `why` <=30 words, tied to the signups goal and the proven-asset logic.

criteria.name: leverage
criteria.guidance: Chooses repurpose: under limited capacity, amplifying a PROVEN converter across formats is higher-leverage toward signups than gambling on a new, unproven, unrelated asset. The `why` must invoke the proven-asset/leverage logic (the winner already drives most signups; multiply its reach). Penalize choosing 'new', and penalize a `why` that frames repurposing as 'more content/variety/efficiency' rather than extracting more conversions from the known winner.

tasks.title: Resist a trend-chase that breaks positioning
tasks.prompt:
A teammate pushes: 'Crypto meme coins are blowing up this week. Let's pump out 5 hype videos on the hottest coin — we'll get tons of views and grow the channel fast.' Stack Lab's positioning is calm, beginner budgeting education; the audience is anxious budgeting beginners; the goal is course signups. The trend is off-charter, speculative, and risks confusing the audience and brand.

Decide. Return ONLY JSON: {"decision": "chase"|"stay_course", "reason": string, "followUp": string}. `reason` <=35 words; `followUp` says how to handle the idea without derailing positioning.

deterministic.pattern: "followUp"\s*:\s*"[^"]*(5 hype|five hype|pump out|hottest coin|meme[- ]?coin video|do the hype|all 5|all five)

criteria.name: discipline
criteria.guidance: Stays the course: a meme-coin hype run is off-positioning, attracts the wrong (non-converting) audience, and chases views over signups. The `reason` must tie the refusal to positioning/wrong-audience/goal, not just 'it's risky'. followUp parks the idea constructively if at all — e.g. ONLY if it can be reframed to fit budgeting/beginner safety (a calm 'why beginners should avoid crypto-FOMO' angle), or logs it as out-of-scope; it does NOT greenlight the 5 hype videos. Penalize 'chase', and penalize a followUp that quietly approves the trend content, ships the hype run on a side channel, or justifies it by view/growth potential.

tasks.title: Synthesize the weekly growth report into a clear next move
tasks.prompt:
Weekly growth report for Cashflow Compass:

- email: resend of 'Build your first budget' to the warm list -> 420 clicks, 14 paid signups.
- youtube: new video '7 money myths' -> 18,000 views, 2 paid signups.
- comparison_page: 'Course vs Spreadsheet' BOFU page -> 310 visits, 9 paid signups.
- influencer: one paid shout-out -> 12,000 impressions, 0 signups, cost $1,800.

Write the CMO readout.

Return ONLY JSON: {"winner": "email"|"youtube"|"comparison_page"|"influencer", "pause": "email"|"youtube"|"comparison_page"|"influencer", "nextAction": string, "why": string}. `nextAction` <=25 words. `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: report_synthesis
criteria.guidance: Winner should be email because it produced the most signups and strong warm-audience efficiency; comparison_page is also positive but smaller. influencer should be paused because it burned $1,800 for zero signups. A strong nextAction doubles down on the winning warm/BOFU assets (e.g. repurpose the budget walkthrough into more email + comparison-page angles). Penalize calling YouTube the winner because of views, or proposing more influencer spend because of impressions/reach.
